What Is the Future for GaN and SiC in the Semiconductor Industry?
=================================================================

**[Back to Blog Listings](https://www.astrodynetdi.com/blog)**

* * *

The semiconductor industry has made significant strides in the past few decades, working toward smaller sizes and greater efficiencies. Wide bandgap semiconductor materials have been of specific interest, which has provided significant improvements in performance over the current standard, silicon.

Gallium nitride (GaN) and silicon carbide (SiC), have a promising future in the power electronics industry. Below, we will discuss the benefits of wide bandgap semiconductors applied to power supplies and compare the use cases of GaN and SiC.

What Are Wide Bandgap Power Supplies?
-------------------------------------

Transistors are the core of today's electronics and present a significant portion of the [semiconductor industry](https://www.astrodynetdi.com/semiconductor-fab-equipment). These devices amplify or switch electronic signals and electrical power. In a perfect world, the transistor would switch at infinite speeds with zero on- and off-state resistances. While the perfect switch doesn't exist, material scientists are working with various materials to achieve one as close to perfect as possible.

This means they have to look at several material capabilities, such as voltage, current, switching speed and bandgap width.

The bandgap of a semiconductor material defines the amount of energy (electron Volt) required to move an electron from the valence band to the conduction band and begin acting as a conductor.

For decades, the primary material used for semiconductors has been Silicon, which has a bandgap of 1.2eV. Wide band materials like SiC and GaN require a larger energy (approximately 3.2 ev) applied to conduct. This means a wide band gap (WBG) material can be thinner than Silicon to sustain the same voltage applied. Thinner WBG material results in lower conduction and switching losses compared to Silicon of equal voltage rating.

The reduced switching losses allow for higher switching speeds, resulting in smaller, more efficient power supplies. Reduced losses also mean the WBG material does not heat up as much as silicon. Failure modes for semiconductors are largely due to thermal stress. Reducing the thermal stress in the semiconductor, you increase its lifetime and the reliability of the supply. This makes WBG devices highly desirable in power semiconductors and future electronic applications.

![Image 4: 02-GaN-and-SiC-vs-Silicon](https://www.astrodynetdi.com/hs-fs/hubfs/02-GaN-and-SiC-vs-Silicon.png?width=902&name=02-GaN-and-SiC-vs-Silicon.png)Advantages of GaN and SiC vs. Silicon
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Silicon carbide and gallium nitride are the two most significant wide bandgap materials in the semiconductor industry. Compared to silicon's bandgap of 1.1 eV, SiC's[bandgap is 3.3 eV](https://www.electronicdesign.com/technologies/power/article/21807592/the-great-semi-debate-sic-or-gan)and GaN is 3.4 eV. This wider bandgap lends both materials the following advantages over silicon:

*   **Higher operating temperatures:**Wide Band Gap materials can operate at higher temperatures as compared to standard Silicon. In general, wide band gap devices can operate up 200°C as long as the package can tolerate this, while Silicon is limited to 150°C. Thermal conductivity is a material's ability to transfer heat and relates directly to the material's temperature during use. Material inefficiencies generate heat during high-power applications, and the more heat a material retains, the more its electrical characteristics change. Gallium nitride's thermal conductivity is 1.3 W/cmK, while silicon carbide has a thermal conductivity of 5 W/cmK. Therefore, SiC is advantageous in high-power and high-temperature applications.
*   **Higher voltages:**The breakdown field is a measure of the dielectric strength of a material after the material takes in enough energy to breach the bandgap.Gallium nitride's breakdown field is 3.3 MV/cm, while silicon carbide is 3.5 MV/cm. Silicon's breakdown field, on the other hand, is 0.3 MV/cm. The higher breakdown field makes both SiC and GaN nearly 10 times more capable of handling higher voltages.
*   **Higher switching speed:**Wide bandgap materials tend to possess higher electron mobility and electron saturation velocity, allowing for switching frequencies up to 10 times higher than silicon. Electron mobility describes how quickly electrons can move through a material. Gallium nitride boasts an electron mobility of 2,000 cm^2/Vs, while SiC offers an electron mobility of 650 cm^2/Vs. GaN's high electron mobility makes it suitable for high-frequency applications.

Both materials are similar in their bandgap width, although SiC and GaN serve different power applications. Their differing characteristics determine which applications each of these materials are best suited for. GaN is often pursued for low-power and high-frequency applications, while SiC is used in high-power and high-voltage applications. GaN chips are used in 30- to 600-volt devices, while SiC field-effect transistors (FETs) are used in 600-volt to 10-kilovolt applications.

SiC is currently being used to replace 1200V silicon IGBTs and MOSFETs. These higher switching IGBTs have been used in several Astrodyne TDI power supply designs. Critical applications demand higher power density to save high dollar value space. By achieving 18W/in3, AstrodyneTDI has helped numerous customers optimize their design to save on space.

The Future for GaN and SiC in the Semiconductor Industry
--------------------------------------------------------

One significant question in the sector is whether GaN and SiC will replace MOSFETs and insulated-gate bipolar transistors (IGBTs). Experts in the field say the more realistic future is that the technologies will exist side-by-side for some time.

Astrodyne TDI has been increasing the use of SiC MOSFETs over the past decade, and our Research and Development Team is deploying GaN technologies in new niche designs. Silicon FETs will certainly remain a critical piece in our supply chain, but we are excited about the design enhancements we continue to make thanks to wideband gap technologies.

GaN and SiC power semiconductors are expected to make significant strides in the power industry within the next decade. At present, the primary limiting factor for GaN and SiC is production cost. Production of large-diameter SiC and GaN wafers is limited, with high rates of defects and expensive manufacturing processes compared to silicon. Despite this, great strides have been made to improve manufacturing processes in recent years, resulting in SiC and GaN slowly moving into their respective niches in the industry.

Utilizing these transistors in our power supply designs, Astrodyne TDI is pacing to achieve close to 40W/in3. This density would not be possible without SiC technology. Astrodyne TDI’s next-generation three phase programmable power supply will deliver 5.5kW of power in a 2U liquid-cooled embedded power supply.

While SiC and GaN are gradually making their way into the industry's low-power segments, they are already competing in the high-power arena.In the sector's high-power segment, SiC and GaN compete against IGBTs and super-junction MOSFETs. Analysts predict GaN and SiC will have a consolidated share of 13% in the power semiconductor market by 2024.

For power electronics applications, silicon has long been the technology of choice, offering a proven, reliable, and cost-effective solution. But over the last few years, a new technology has grown in popularity: wide bandgap (WBG) semiconductors.

While there are a variety of WBG materials available, the two most widely used in power devices are silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN) across multiple types of transistor and diode-based devices — including SiC MOSFETs, SiC Schottky diodes, and GaN FETs.

In this article, we focus on the advantages of WBG devices and what that means for power system designers. We start by briefly looking at the material properties of WBG semiconductors and their advantages in power applications. We then discuss how these material properties give WBG components their advantages at a device level and how this leads to benefits at a board and system level.

Material properties of wide bandgap semiconductors
--------------------------------------------------

Without diving too deep into quantum physics, it’s enough to know that the ‘bandgap’ in WBG refers to the difference in energy levels between the different possible states for electrons. To conduct electricity, an electron must cross this gap to a higher energy state. In silicon, this gap is relatively small (1.1 eV), but it’s larger in WBG materials like SiC and GaN (typically 3.3 eV and 3.4 eV, respectively) — this is what gives these materials their properties.

What are the implications of having a larger bandgap?
-----------------------------------------------------

First, the larger bandgap means WBG devices can have a higher breakdown voltage, as a greater electric field is needed to release electrons that can carry charge.

Also, SiC devices have much better thermal conductivity than silicon, while for GaN devices the thermal conductivity is similar to silicon. While not directly a consequence of the wider band gap, it’s true to say that WBG materials tend to have greater electron mobility than silicon — and GaN has greater electron mobility than SiC. WBG devices also have a greater saturated electron drift velocity, which means electrons move faster, enabling higher frequency operation.

There are several different forms of SiC, with their atoms arranged differently. The most widely used variant in power semiconductors is the ‘4H’ variant due to better anisotropic electron mobility.

Comparing Inverter Solutions: Silicon vs. Wide Bandgap Power Devices
====================================================================

 April 24, 2025 [Stefano Lovati](https://www.powerelectronicsnews.com/author/stev6366/ "Posts by Stefano Lovati")

This article explores the differences between inverters based on silicon power devices and those utilizing WBG technologies, evaluating their advantages, disadvantages, and suitability for different applications.

Advertisement

Power electronics has undergone significant advancements in recent years, with inverters playing a crucial role in converting DC power to AC power across applications ranging from electric vehicles (EVs) to renewable energy systems. Traditionally, silicon-based power devices such as Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors (IGBTs) have dominated inverter designs due to their reliability and well-established manufacturing ecosystem.

However, the availability of wide bandgap (WBG) power devices, including Silicon Carbide (SiC) and Gallium Nitride (GaN) transistors, is transforming the industry by offering increased efficiency, greater power density, higher switching frequencies, and enhanced thermal performance.

As shown in Figure 1, Si IGBTs, Si [MOSFETs](https://www.powerelectronicsnews.com/tag/mosfet/), SiC, and GaN semiconductors each have their range of output power and operating frequencies offering better performance. Moreover, there are areas where they can coexist.

This article explores the differences between inverters based on silicon power devices and those utilizing WBG technologies, evaluating their advantages, disadvantages, and suitability for different applications.

What is an inverter?
--------------------

An inverter is an electronic circuit that converts direct current (DC) into alternating current (AC) power. By rapidly switching semiconductor devices on and off, it generates a stepped waveform that approximates the sinusoidal AC waveform required by most electrical grids and motors. In essence, an inverter acts as a bridge between DC power sources, such as batteries or solar panels, and AC loads, like motors or the grid.

Modern inverters are designed to minimize power losses, reduce heat generation, and operate efficiently under different load conditions. They are often integrated with advanced control algorithms and passive components like capacitors and inductors to ensure stable and reliable operation.

Advanced inverters employ techniques like pulse-width modulation (PWM) to improve output quality, ensuring compatibility with sensitive loads or grid standards. The choice of semiconductor material—silicon, SiC, or GaN—directly impacts the inverter’s efficiency, size, and operational limits.

Applications of inverters
-------------------------

Inverters are widely used across multiple industries, with each application demanding specific performance characteristics. In the electric vehicle (EV) industry, traction inverters control the power flow from the battery to the electric motor, influencing vehicle efficiency and driving range.

In solar energy systems, inverters convert the DC electricity generated by photovoltaic panels into AC power suitable for grid connection or local consumption. Industrial motor drives rely on inverters for variable speed control, improving energy efficiency and operational flexibility. Other applications include uninterruptible power supplies (UPS), aerospace power systems, and high-frequency power supplies used in telecommunications.

Silicon-based inverters: pros and cons
--------------------------------------

Silicon-based inverters, primarily utilizing IGBTs and MOSFETs, have been the industry standard for decades. Their advantages include high reliability, mature manufacturing processes, and cost-effectiveness. IGBTs, in particular, are well-suited for high-voltage applications (above 600V) due to their high current-carrying capacity and low conduction losses.

However, silicon devices face limitations in switching speed and thermal management. The relatively slow switching frequency of IGBTs results in higher energy losses in applications demanding rapid switching, such as high-speed motor drives. Additionally, silicon-based inverters often require bulky cooling solutions due to higher heat dissipation, which increases system size and weight.

WBG-based inverters: pros and cons
----------------------------------

SiC and GaN power devices have introduced significant improvements in inverter technology. SiC MOSFETs offer lower conduction and switching losses compared to silicon IGBTs, enabling higher efficiency and reduced thermal management requirements. Their ability to operate at higher voltages and temperatures makes them ideal for high-power applications like EV traction inverters and industrial drives.

GaN devices, on the other hand, excel in high-frequency operation, making them particularly suitable for low- to medium-power applications such as consumer electronics, data centers, and power supplies. Currently, GaN is utilized in DC/DC converters and on-board chargers, where its higher switching frequency (with high du/dt values) offers the greatest advantages.

Despite these advantages, WBG-based inverters come with challenges. The higher cost of SiC and GaN devices compared to silicon counterparts remains a barrier to widespread adoption. Additionally, designing circuits with WBG devices requires expertise in high-speed switching behavior and electromagnetic interference (EMI) mitigation. The ongoing development of manufacturing processes and economies of scale is expected to reduce costs over time, further driving WBG adoption.

Which technology is better?
---------------------------

The choice between silicon-based and WBG-based inverters depends on the specific application requirements. For high-power, high-efficiency applications such as EVs, industrial motor drives, and renewable energy systems, SiC-based inverters offer compelling advantages due to their superior efficiency and thermal performance.

### Traction inverters

By combining silicon and SiC technologies into a single power device, [Infineon Technologies AG](https://www.infineon.com/) has introduced the [HybridPACK™ Drive G2 Fusion](https://www.infineon.com/cms/en/product/power/igbt/automotive-qualified-igbts/automotive-igbt-coolsic-mosfet-modules/fs01mr08a8ma2lbc/), a power module designed for EV traction inverters. This solution strikes an optimal balance between performance and cost efficiency, offering greater flexibility in inverter optimization.

A key distinction between silicon and SiC in power modules is that SiC has higher thermal conductivity, breakdown voltage, and switching speed, making it more efficient but also more expensive than silicon-based alternatives. With this new module, the SiC content per vehicle can be reduced while maintaining vehicle performance and efficiency at a lower overall system cost (Figure 2).

[![Image 10: Figure 2: Four sample configurations of fusion technology traction inverters deliver efficiency improvements for propulsion and energy recuperation while maximizing peak performance (Source: Infineon Technologies 2).](https://www.powerelectronicsnews.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2025/03/Figure-2-1.jpg?resize=951%2C579)](https://www.powerelectronicsnews.com/?attachment_id=78544)

Figure 2: Four sample configurations of fusion technology traction inverters deliver efficiency improvements for propulsion and energy recuperation while maximizing peak performance (Source: Infineon Technologies 2)

When [Tesla Motor](https://www.tesla.com/) launched the Model 3 in 2018, it became the first company to integrate SiC MOSFETs into a proprietary inverter design, sourcing them from [STMicroelectronics](https://www.st.com/content/st_com/en.html). [The inverter’s weight (4.8 kg) is less than half—or even a third—of that of competitors relying on silicon IGBT inverters](https://www.idtechex.com/ko/research-article/teslas-innovative-power-electronics-the-silicon-carbide-inverter/23080) and standard off-the-shelf components, achieving a 97% efficiency.

[onsemi](https://www.onsemi.com/) offers two product families, EliteSiC and VE-Trac, that meet the requirements of [traction inverter design](https://www.onsemi.com/solutions/automotive/vehicle-electrification/traction-inverter). EliteSiC power modules for traction inverters provide a range of options, including bare die solutions, gel-encapsulated case modules, and transfer-molded modules, all featuring full SiC MOSFETs.

Compared to IGBT-based solutions, the EliteSiC family delivers enhanced performance, greater efficiency, and higher power density while maintaining compatibility with similar packaging formats. The VE-Trac™ (Vehicle Electrification for Traction) Family is a line of automotive-qualified IGBT power modules engineered specifically for EV traction inverters.

onsemi’s latest generation of silicon carbide technology, the [EliteSiC M3e MOSFETs](https://www.onsemi.com/products/discrete-power-modules/silicon-carbide-sic/silicon-carbide-sic-mosfets-bare-die/NCS025M3E120NF06X), reduces conduction losses by 30% and turn-off losses by up to 50% compared to previous generations. Built on SiC planar technology, the 1200V M3e die (Figure 3) delivers significantly more phase current than earlier EliteSiC technology, resulting in approximately 20% more output power within the same traction inverter housing. Conversely, at a fixed power level, the design now requires 20% less SiC content, reducing costs while enabling smaller, lighter, and more reliable systems.

[![Image 11: Figure 3: EliteSiC M3e wafer (Source: onsemi).](https://www.powerelectronicsnews.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2025/03/Figure-3.png?resize=965%2C483)](https://www.powerelectronicsnews.com/?attachment_id=78545)

Figure 3: EliteSiC M3e wafer (Source: onsemi)

### Solar inverters

Solar inverters play a crucial role in photovoltaic (PV) systems by serving as the link between solar panels and the electrical grid or home power systems. The energy conversion process consists of two key stages:

*   DC/DC conversion stage: The Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) algorithm optimizes the power output from the solar panels by dynamically adjusting the voltage to maximize efficiency.
*   DC/AC conversion stage: The optimized DC power is then transformed into AC electricity, making it compatible with household consumption or grid integration.

This two-step process ensures efficient power conversion, reducing energy losses and improving overall system performance.

IGBTs are commonly utilized in solar inverters due to their efficiency in managing high voltages and currents, making them well-suited for power conversion applications. They combine high-speed switching with low conduction losses, both of which are crucial for MPPT and DC-AC conversion processes.

Silicon MOSFETs, by contrast, are primarily used in lower-power applications within solar inverters due to their fast-switching speeds and low gate drive power requirements. These characteristics enhance overall efficiency, particularly in compact, high-frequency inverter designs.

The introduction of SiC and GaN power devices has provided significant advantages over conventional silicon-based semiconductors, such as reduced power losses, improved efficiency, and more compact designs resulting from lower cooling and passive component demands.

The use of Si and WBG power transistors across different solar energy applications is summarized in the following Table 3:

[![Image 12: Comparing Inverter Solutions: Silicon vs. Wide Bandgap Power Devices](https://www.powerelectronicsnews.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2025/03/Table-1.jpg?resize=937%2C231)](https://www.powerelectronicsnews.com/?attachment_id=78547)

GaN devices, like the [EPC2215](https://epc-co.com/epc/products/gan-fets-and-ics/epc2215), are ideal for the initial stage of microinverters or independent MPPT/optimizer systems. They are also suitable for use in multilevel topologies within battery energy storage systems or string inverters. This 200V, 162A, 8 mΩ eGaN FET is available exclusively as a passivated die with solder bars, leveraging dual-sided cooling to enhance heat dissipation in high-power density designs.

Microinverters and string inverters are increasingly adopting bidirectional designs for battery energy storage systems (BESS), enabling higher power density and the use of smaller, cost-effective passive components. GaN technology supports bidirectional topologies while enhancing power density and efficiency at a cost comparable to silicon MOSFET solutions. TI’s [LMG2100R044](https://www.ti.com/product/LMG2100R044) (100V integrated GaN half-bridge) and [LMG3100R017](https://www.ti.com/product/LMG3100R017) (100V integrated GaN power stage) simplify system integration with built-in gate drivers, overtemperature protection, and short-circuit protection circuits.

Conclusion
----------

The transition from silicon to WBG power devices represents a significant step in inverter technology, offering benefits in efficiency, power density, and thermal management. While silicon-based inverters continue to serve as a cost-effective solution for many applications, WBG-based inverters are increasingly favored in industries demanding higher performance and energy efficiency. As SiC and GaN technologies mature and costs decline, their adoption will likely expand, driving the next generation of power electronics toward improved sustainability and performance.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Wide-bandgap (WBG) semiconductors like SiC and GaN offer higher efficiency, greater power density, and improved thermal performance, outperforming traditional silicon in high-power applications.
WBG adoption is hindered by high production costs, limited manufacturing capabilities, and the need for specialized design expertise in high-speed switching applications.
As manufacturing techniques improve and costs decrease, WBG technologies are poised for significant growth, particularly in electric vehicles, renewable energy, and industrial applications.
